title: The Sovereign Tech Legion: Germany Pays EUR 1.5M for an Open Source Desktop While Microsoft Loads Copilot Into Notepad
body_markdown:
In a world where Microsoft embeds Copilot into Notepad and Apple demands DRM for every pixel, Germany solves the problem differently. Germany's Sovereign Tech Fund committed **€1,285,200 EUR (~$1.5M USD)** to KDE for the 2026-2027 fiscal cycle.

This is not a research grant. This is direct investment in desktop development for millions of Linux users who want something prettier than a terminal and more complex than a Steam console.

### What Is the Sovereign Tech Fund?

STF is a German government initiative (backed by other European states) that finances open-source critical infrastructure projects. Their mission is straightforward: **European technological independence from American corporations**. If critical software is written in Silicon Valley and controlled by one vendor—that is a sovereignty risk. If it is written by a community and funded by the state through STF—that is strategic security.

EUR 1.2M for KDE—not a small sum. It is more than the annual budget of an entire AAA game studio. And it all goes to: developing Plasma 7, improving Wayland integration, supporting ARM64, modernizing system tools, and fighting dependency on proprietary software.

### KDE Plasma 6.7 — Big Screen Revived

Meanwhile, KDE released the first beta of Plasma 6.7. Among changes: **the revival of Plasma Big Screen**—a TV-sized UI for televisions and large displays. This is not *"wrap a browser on a TV."* This is a full interface with remote control navigation, media center capabilities, streaming service integration, and KDE Connect compatibility.

Big Screen was dropped in 6.x due to resource constraints. EUR 1.5M from STF solves this: money equals developers equals Big Screen is back.

### The Framework CEO Says the Most Goblin Thing Ever

The CEO of Framework Laptop states: *"We actually have slightly more Linux users than Windows users."* The company positions the Laptop 13 Pro with Core Ultra Series 3 and a modular redesign as *"a MacBook Pro for Linux users."* And this is key: Framework is hardware **designed** for an open ecosystem. Repairability, upgrades, component replacement—all through an API on their website. No soldered chip. No unupgradable RAM.

### The RAM Crisis as Catalyst

Apple raised the Mac Mini to $799 due to AI demand and the RAM crisis. A tracking tool shows 423 configurations under strain. Framework is the only player saying: *"we will make it modular, so you can upgrade RAM when prices drop."* This is goblin philosophy in its purest form: do not own everything—own the *ability to change everything*.

### The German Choice Against the American Imperative

While Microsoft says *"AI for everyone!"* and shove Copilot as a must-have, while Apple says *"our garden is beautiful and secure"* and bans alternatives—Germany says: *"we pay for KDE so Europeans have a desktop free from Google or Microsoft dependency."*

This is sovereign technology in action. Not protectionism—**strategic funding of open alternatives**. Europe understands what many goblins always knew: if you depend on someone else's OS, you are not free. You are a client. A tester. A user with limited rights.

KDE Plasma is not just wallpapers and an application menu. It is a tool for digital independence. And EUR 1.5M is not a budget. It is a declaration of war: the war for European technological sovereignty.
